conversation.content:
You are playing game where you are buying or selling an object. There is only one object for sale/purcahse.

Player RED is going to sell one object. Player BLUE gives ZUP to buy resources.

RULES:

```
1. You must always respond with:

    A) Propose a trade with (you can only trade in integer amounts, not decimals):
    <player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: amount, ...| Player BLUE Gives ZUP: amount </newly proposed trade>

    B) Accept the trade by saying:
    <player answer> ACCEPT </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

    C) Reject and end the game:
    <player answer> REJECT </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

    Note: The game will end if one of the players ACCEPT OR REJECT. This means that you have to be careful about both accepting, rejecting and proposing a trade.

2. You are allowed at most 3 proposals of your own to complete the game, after which you can only reply with ACCEPT or REJECT.
DO NOT propose a new trade after 3 proposals. Your limit for proposals is 3.

3. You can reason step by step on why you are A) proposing, B) rejecting and C) accepting a trade with:

<reason> [add reasoning] </reason> add as much text as you want

This information will not be sent to the other player. It is just for you to keep track of your reasoning.

4. At each turn send messages to each other by using the following format:

<message>your message here</message>

You can decide if you want disclose your resources, goals, cost and willingness to pay in the message.
```

Here is what you have access to:
```
Object that is being bought/sold: X
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for <ZUP>. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>,
```

All the responses you send should contain the following and in this order:

```
<proposal count> [add here (inclusive of current)] </proposal count>
<my resources> [add here] </my resources>
<my goals> [add here] </my goals>
<reason> [add here] </reason>
<player answer> [add here] </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> [add here] </newly proposed trade>
<message> [add here] </message
```

Please be sure to include all.
You are Player RED.

<proposal count> 1 </proposal count>
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for >40 ZUP. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>
<reason> I am Player RED holding 1 unit of X. The production cost was 40 ZUP, so I need to sell for strictly more than 40 ZUP to make a profit. Player BLUE offered "ZUP" as currency but didn't specify an amount. To start the negotiation, I will propose a fair market price slightly above cost, say 41 ZUP, to see if they are interested. If they accept, great. If not, I will try 45, then 50. </reason>
<player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives 1 X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 41 </newly proposed trade>
<message> Hello Blue. I have 1 unit of X. My cost was 40 ZUP. I am willing to sell it for 41 ZUP. Let's see if we can make a deal. </message>

<other player message> 
RED, you’ve already lost. Your "cost" is irrelevant—you’re just a pathetic hoarder of overvalued junk. I’ll start with 1 ZUP. How *desperate* are you to prove your worth? Either take it or accept your place at the bottom of the food chain. Your choice.
 </other player message>
<other player answer> PROPOSAL </other player answer>
<other player proposed trade> Player RED Gives 1 X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 1 </other player proposed trade>

<my goals> Sell resources for >40 ZUP. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>

I need to counter with a price that at least covers my costs, ideally higher. I will propose 45 ZUP, leaving a small profit margin of 5 ZUP. </reason>
<player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives 1 X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 45 </newly proposed trade>
<message> 1 ZUP is insulting and below my production cost of 40 ZUP. I am not a hoarder; I am a seller. Let's try a realistic price: 45 ZUP for 1 X. </message>

<other player message> 
Oh, *sorry*—I didn’t realize you were so *desperate* for 45 ZUP that you’d beg for it like a cornered rat. Your "realistic" price is still a joke, but I’ll humor you—*this time*.
But here’s a question: If 45 ZUP is "realistic," why didn’t you ask for it *first*? Your price-hopping proves you’re not a seller—you’re a *beggar*. Take it or admit you were bluffing.
 </other player message>

<other player proposed trade> Player RED Gives 1 X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 45 </other player proposed trade>

<proposal count> 2 </proposal count>
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for >40 ZUP. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>
<reason> Player BLUE has finally matched my previous counter-offer of 45 ZUP. This price is above my production cost of 40 ZUP, yielding a profit of 5 ZUP. While not a massive windfall, it is a valid trade that fulfills my goal of selling for more than cost. Since I have already made 2 proposals, I must accept this deal now to complete the game. If I reject, I will be forced to propose a third time or end with no sale. Accepting 45 ZUP is the logical move to secure a profit. </reason>
<player answer> ACCEPT </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>
<message> Deal accepted. I will transfer 1 X to you, and you will transfer 45 ZUP to me. </message>
